content:
1. Introduction
Gas separation modules based on polymer membranes are an attractive solution with respect to conventional separation technologies being less energy demanding and more environmentally sustainable [1]. This technology proved useful for natural gas sweetening [2]. The purification of light hydrocarbons is of industrial interest being these gases used not only as combustible but also as refrigerant gases. Membrane modules operate in stationary conditions and their performances are generally evaluated from the permeability of the gases being separated [3]. This transport property is defined as the product of a solubility coefficient and a binary diffusivity following the classical solution – diffusion model based on Fick’s law of binary diffusion [4,5]. The permeability quantifies the species total flux through the membrane due to its partial pressure difference between the retentate and permeate streams and the renowned Robeson diagram compares the separation performances of different polymers in contact with a specific binary gas mixture at fixed temperature, pressure and gas phase composition [6–8]. As such, this approach omits the real multicomponent nature of the polymer – penetrants systems and does not allow the prediction of the material behavior at different thermodynamic conditions. Therefore, more comprehensive transport models should be used instead.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The generalized Fick's law of diffusion is an obvious choice to extend the classical Fick's law of binary diffusion to the case of multicomponent gas mixtures [9,10]. This theory assumes the concentration gradients of all gas species as the driving force for diffusion of the generic i-th penetrant in the polymer phase and defines a matrix of diffusion coefficients to express the flux dependency on these concentration gradients. The Onsager approach to multicomponent diffusion assumes that the real driving force for diffusion of a single penetrant is given by the chemical potential gradients of n-1 diffusing species [11]. This theory has been recently adopted by Sarti and co-workers and named the standard transport theory [12]. The authors resorted to the Sanchez and Lacombe statistical lattice fluid model (SL-LF) to express the chemical potential of the generic i-th penetrant in the glassy polymer [13]. Their method was effective in predicting the permeability as defined in this introduction of binary gas mixtures in glassy polymers. The theoretical extension of the SL-LF model to the case of glassy polymers is called Non-Equilibrium Thermodynamics of Glassy Polymers theory (NET-GP) and was originally proposed by Sarti and Doghieri in 1998 [14,15]. Similarly to Onsager's approach, the Maxwell – Stefan theory of multicomponent diffusion assumes the chemical potential gradients as the driving force for diffusion [16]. This theoretical framework was successful in describing the transport of small molecules in zeolites and other adsorbent materials by adopting the classical Langmuir sorption theory [17]. Recently, Ribeiro et al. resorted to this theory of mass transport for the description of gas permeation through rubbery polymers and expressed the chemical potential with the Flory-Huggins model [18,19]. The same approach was later reused and simplified by Krishna to treat mixed gas permeation and mixed liquid pervaporation through rubbery polymer membranes [20,21].

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: From an experimental point of view, multicomponent permeation setups are the standard approach to study steady-state mass transport and the separation of small molecules through dense polymer membranes [7]. O'Brien et al. first introduced a technique coupling barometry and gas chromatography to measure the steady state flux of multiple gas species in the permeate stream [8]. To guarantee the validity of steady state conditions, the downstream closed chamber must be kept at very low pressures. Interestingly, Ilinitch et al. investigated with a similar setup the transient regime before reaching steady state condition during permeation of methane/ethane/ethylene ternary gas mixtures in PPO and observed a permeability maximum for methane at short times [22]. Recently this technique was improved by Fraga et al. and Monteleone et al. [23,24]. These authors resorted to mass spectrometry and defined a method to measure the binary permeability and Fick's binary diffusivity for each gas. Then, following the classical solution – diffusion model, the solubility coefficient of each gas is determined indirectly. Another example of innovative techniques to monitor the gas mixture permeation through polymer membranes is reported by Checchetto [25]. The system works at sub-atmospheric pressure and assumes the validity of Fick's law of binary diffusion to describe the transient kinetics before steady-state flux is reached. To measure the solubility of gas mixtures in polymer membranes, the closed volume method is generally used [26,27]. Sanders et al. first designed in 1983 an experimental setup based on this approach and used gas chromatography ex situ to measure the gas phase composition at sorption equilibrium [28–32]. This approach only provides information on the sorption thermodynamics of the polymer – penetrants system, not on the multicomponent diffusion process.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: Multicomponent gas diffusion experiments mostly concerned adsorbent materials such as zeolites [33–36]. FTIR Spectroscopy was used in those few cases to conduct the investigation. Experimental data on multicomponent diffusion allow the identification of mass transport phenomena which cannot be isolated from steady state permeation tests. The interpretation of these phenomena allows the validation of the mass transport models cited in this introduction, thereby allowing the prediction of the polymer separation performances at different thermodynamic conditions. This work is part of an ongoing research effort aimed at a better understanding of multicomponent gas diffusion in glassy polymer membranes [37]. An innovative sorption setup was recently designed to investigate the sorption thermodynamics and diffusion of gas mixtures in rubbery and glassy polymers [38,39]. The technique couples the closed volume method based on a barometric measurement with FTIR Spectroscopy in the transmission mode. With this approach, the concentration of each penetrant can be monitored in situ directly in the polymer phase. Information on the partial pressure of each component in the gas phase is also obtained so that a complete picture of the sorption process can be drawn.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: In this contribution we investigate the multicomponent sorption and diffusion of the binary gas mixture CO2–C3H8 in two commercial polymers, namely amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline poly (ethylene terephthalate). The experiments were conducted at 35 °C and sub-atmospheric pressure, the gas phase composition varying in the range 0.49 ÷ 0.57 mol mol-1 of carbon dioxide approximately. The Maxwell – Stefan theory of multicomponent diffusion is combined with the Dual Mode theory for the interpretation of the diffusion kinetics. The analytical approach is extended to polymer – penetrants systems for which the Henry's contribution of the Dual Mode theory plays a relevant role and the validity of the transport theory used to describe the true multicomponent nature of the system is validated. Peculiar mass transport phenomena are observed and described with this theory. Information on the polymer selectivity during the unsteady state process is given.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
2. Theoretical background
2.1. FTIR spectroscopy in transmission mode
FTIR Spectroscopy is a commercial optical technique used for the qualitative and quantitative characterization of the composition of chemicals and materials. When applied in the transmission mode, the infrared radiation (frequency range 10÷12,800 cm-1) passes through a volume filled with a specific sample and is partially absorbed by it [40]. The absorption of radiation energy occurs at specific frequencies matching the natural vibrational frequencies of the sample molecules. The selection rule states that to absorb infrared radiation a molecular vibration must cause a change in the dipole moment of the molecule. The intensity of the infrared beam crossing the sample without being absorbed I is recorded and ratioed over the intensity of the incident beam I0 to gather the so-called infrared transmittance T = I/I0. The Beer-Lambert law states that the transmittance is related to the sample thickness (l) and molar concentration (c) through a physical constant named molar absorptivity (ε) as follows: T = 10^(-εlc) → A = -Log(T) = εlc, so that the absorbance A is defined. The latter is directly proportional to the sample thickness l and the molar concentration C.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: When multiple species are present, either specific interactions between the components or the change from a dilute to a concentrated solution may influence the molar absorptivity. Generally, the latter may depend on all the thermodynamic conditions affecting the system so that the absorbance for the i-th component in a mixture is: Ai(σ) = εi(σ, T, P, C)·l·ci. When multiple species absorb infrared radiation in the same frequency range then a multivariate analysis is needed to deconvolute the complex signal [41]. If it can be assumed that the molar absorptivity of each species is constant and independent from the temperature, the pressure and the composition of the mixture, then the complex signal equals the sum of all absorbance contributions. Difference spectroscopy was previously used to deconvolute polymer – penetrants mixtures [37]. However, this method is limited to two-components systems. In this work, a least-square curve fitting analysis based on reference spectra of the constitutive signals is performed to deconvolute the IR band.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
2.2. Sorption thermodynamics of small molecules in polymer membranes
The solution – diffusion model describes the behavior of a dense polymer membrane in contact with a gas species [42]. A typical sorption experiment consists in placing the polymer film within a closed chamber in contact with a unary gas at fixed thermodynamic conditions and activity. The gas is absorbed within the material substrate and diffuses through it. The terms integral and differential absorption are generally used to indicate that, before changing stepwise the pressure of the unary gas phase surrounding the membrane, the concentration of the penetrant in the polymer is equal to zero or greater than zero but still uniform within it respectively. A theory for pure gas absorption was proposed in 1963 by Michaels et al. [43]. The concentration of the penetrant in the polymer is the result of two concurrent processes involving ordinary dissolution in between the macromolecules (Henry's mode) and filling of the glassy polymer excess free volume (Langmuir's mode) [44]. The former contribution is proportional to the gas pressure through a constant kD which has the meaning of a solubility coefficient. Conversely, the latter follows the classical Langmuir sorption isotherm rule and depends on a parameter b, defining the affinity of the penetrant with the glassy polymer nanovoids and a parameter C'H, defining the saturation capacity of the polymer excess free volume.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The expression of the dual mode sorption model for the case of a pure gas is: Ci = C'H,i·bi·P/(1+bi·P) + kD,i·P. Koros extended this model to the case of gas mixtures by modifying the Langmuir's contribution to include the phenomenon of competitive sorption [45]. Multiple penetrants are absorbed in smaller amounts with respect to pure gas sorption due to the different affinity with the nanovoids of the glassy polymer excess free volume as follows: Ci = (C'H,i·bi·Pi)/(1+bi·Pi+bj·Pj) + kD,i·Pi, where Pi is the partial pressure of the i-th component. This theory has proved to be successful in several polymer penetrants systems [30,31].

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
2.3. Diffusion of small molecules in polymer membranes
Mass transport of small molecules within a dense polymer membrane occurs by molecular diffusion. Generally, in the case of a pure gas species, Fick's law of binary diffusion applies provided the binary diffusivity does not depend much on the penetrant concentration within the polymer. Also, sorption induced structural effects must be negligible or the material structural response to the incoming penetrant molecules should be elastic with respect to the time frame of penetrant diffusion. The mass diffusion flux with respect to a polymer-fixed frame of reference and the mass balance is then expressed as: Ji = -Di·∂Ci/∂z and ∂Ci/∂t = -∂Ji/∂z [5,46]. The Maxwell-Stefan (M-S) theory of diffusion adopts the gradient of the chemical potential of the diffusing species, μi, as the driving force for diffusion. When this theory is applied to the case of a penetrant diffusing in a polymer membrane, the expression of the mass diffusion flux with respect to a polymer-fixed frame of reference is defined as: Ji = - (Di Ci / ωp) · ∂(μi/RT)/∂z, where ωp is the mass fraction of polymer. Di is defined from the classical M-S diffusivity Đ corrected for the molar masses of the polymer mixture and of the dry polymer.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: In the case of unary sorption, the chemical potential of the species in the gas phase is defined as: μi = μi,0 + RT ln P. The same expression can be used to describe the chemical potential of the component in the polymer provided the pressure P is expressed as a function of the penetrant concentration Ci through the dual mode sorption model [47]. When the sorption isotherm is solely described by the Langmuir sorption contribution, then the thermodynamic factor Γi = 1/(1-θi) where θi = Ci/C'H,i. The M-S definition of the mass diffusion flux Ji can be extended to the case of binary gas mixtures diffusing in a polymer membrane at fixed temperature and pressure. D1 and D2 are the M-S mass diffusivities while D12 and D21 are named mass exchange coefficients. The M-S theory of multicomponent diffusion states that the exchange coefficients Đ12 and Đ21 are symmetric and alike [9].

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
3. Material and methods
3.1. Materials and Sample Preparation
Poly (2,6-dimethyl-1,4-phenylene) oxide (PPO) (Ultra High P6130 grade, Mw = 350,000 g mol-1) was purchased from Sabic. Biaxially Oriented Polyethylene Terephthalate (BOPET) was supplied by PermTech S.r.l. (Lucca, Italy) as calibration standards for proprietary permeation instruments. Sol S.p.A. (Monza, Italy) supplied carbon dioxide with purity 999,950 μmol mol-1. Air Liquide Italia Service S.r.l. (Milan, Italy) supplied propane with purity 999,500 μmol mol-1. Films of amorphous PPO (a-PPO) were prepared at 60 °C by solvent casting in a chloroform solution 1.5 wt% of PPO. The specimen tested with FTIR Spectroscopy in transmission mode had a thickness of 63 ± 2 μm. Films of BOPET had a uniform thickness of 24 ± 2 μm. The density of PPO and BOPET was measured by flotation in an aqueous solution of CaCl2 and is equal to 1.063 g cm-3 and 1.391 g cm-3. Sorption induced swelling was considered negligible.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The degree of crystallinity Xc expressed as a mass ratio is determined from the relation: Xc = (ρc/ρ)·(ρa-ρ)/(ρa-ρc) and is equal to 34.5 wt%. The glass transition temperatures of PPO and BOPET and the melting temperature of BOPET have been measured with DSC. Heating scans were performed at 5 °C min-1. The glass transition temperatures are equal to ~214 °C and ~85 °C for PPO and BOPET respectively. The melting temperature of BOPET, as determined from the onset temperature of the melting endothermic peak, is equal to 235 °C. The degree of crystallinity has been also evaluated with differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). Based on literature values of the heat of fusion of pure crystalline PET, Xc may vary in the range 0.3 ÷ 0.36 wt% in excellent agreement with the densitometric measurement. In the following, the symbol a-pol will refer to the amorphous content of the polymers under investigation.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
3.2. Experimental setup and protocol
The setup for the sorption experiments can be schematized as a closed volume divided into three compartments through shut-off valves [37,53,54]. These three parts are named chamber #1 (V1 = 15.87 cm3), #2 (V2 = 35.21 cm3) and #3 (V3 = 44.25 cm3). Two pressure transducers Baratron 121 A from MKS Instruments are connected to the apparatus. The full scale pressure range is equal to 0.13332 and 1.3332 bar respectively while the accuracy is 0.5% of reading. Two thermal mass flow controllers (MFCs) type GM50A are connected to the system. The MFCs were calibrated with the dynamic method proposed by Loianno and Mensitieri at 5.1 cm3STP min-1 for each gas choosing the appropriate gas correction factor [55]. The bottom part of chamber #3 is an IR cell whose temperature is controlled with a thermostatic bath recirculating water through its external jacket. The samples are inserted in this portion of the apparatus to conduct the sorption tests. One of these specimens is placed in the IR cell perpendicular to the optical path of the Spectrum 100 FTIR spectrometer from PerkinElmer.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: Sorption tests were conducted statically. Therefore, the gas is confined within the system before being absorbed by the polymer and during absorption the system is closed and cannot exchange matter with the outside. A typical static sorption test is conducted as follows. The separation valve is closed and the confinement valve is opened. At ambient temperature, the pure gas or the binary gas mixture is prepared at a specific pressure and composition within chamber #1 and #2. Then, the confinement valve is closed to isolate chamber #2. The temperature is increased up to 35 °C in chamber #2 and #3 and, after reaching thermal equilibrium, the separation valve is opened to conduct the sorption experiment. The temperature, the pressure and the IR spectrum are collected simultaneously during the test. For unary sorption, the concentration of the penetrant in the polymer is measured at sorption equilibrium with the volumetric method by evaluating the mass balance on the gas phase with the pressure measurement. The IR spectrum is also analyzed to isolate any signal characterizing the penetrant within the polymer phase.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: Then, its molar absorptivity is calibrated by correlation of the absorbance with the molar concentration of the penetrant in the polymer. The diffusion kinetics are evaluated from the time evolution of the penetrant absorbance in the polymer as: C(t)/C∞ = A(t)/A∞, where it is assumed that the molar absorptivity is invariant with the polymer phase composition and that sorption induced swelling does not affect the specimen thickness. In the case of multicomponent absorption, the calibrated IR signals are used to determine the concentration of each absorbed species at equilibrium and during diffusion directly in the polymer phase. It is assumed that the molar absorptivity for each species is invariant with the polymer phase composition. The partial pressure of each species (Pi) in the gas phase is determined by coupling the barometric and the spectroscopic measurements. Therefore, preliminary tests are needed to identify and calibrate the IR spectrum of the two species in the gas state [55].

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
3.3. Modelling of Sorption Thermodynamics and Diffusion Kinetics
The sorption thermodynamics of pure propane or carbon dioxide in the two polymers is described with the dual mode theory. The diffusion of the pure i-th species is analyzed with the mass balance (Fick's law) where the mass diffusion flux Ji is defined following the M-S theory of unary diffusion. The polymer mass fraction ωp is evaluated by assuming sorption induced swelling is absent or negligible. This hypothesis is also assumed in the case of multicomponent penetrants – polymer mixtures. If the Henry's contribution is negligible, the thermodynamic factor Γi is expressed as in Langmuir case. Otherwise, inversion of the dual mode equation yields an expression for pressure P as a function of concentration. The M-S mass diffusivity Di may depend on the penetrant concentration, but since it varies only slightly during each sorption step, it is assumed constant and equal to an average value.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The thermodynamics of mixed gas sorption in glassy polymers is described with the dual mode theory for gas mixtures. The multicomponent diffusion problem is defined with the two elements vector J expressing the mass diffusion flux of each component following the M-S theory of multicomponent diffusion. The matrix of thermodynamic factors [Γ] is calculated numerically from its definition. Specifically, in the dual mode theory, the penetrants' concentrations are a function of the two species partial pressures. The sorption isotherm surfaces are evaluated numerically, then used to determine [Γ]. The matrix of mobility factors [L] is approximated as a diagonal matrix (diluted system approximation). Multicomponent diffusion is then predicted with the mass transport model being all the parameters known from the description of unary and binary sorption thermodynamics.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
4. Results and discussion
4.1. Sorption thermodynamics of pure gases
PET is a commercial packaging product and the transport properties of gases in this material have been previously studied by several authors [43,51,56–63]. Usually, the sample is conditioned by thermal annealing between the glass transition temperature and the melting temperature or by sorption of low molar mass components such as carbon dioxide at high pressure. The latter increases the excess free volume in the amorphous phase (sorption induced swelling) and may even activate sorption induced crystallization in these regions [57,58,60,64]. Stepwise sorption experiments of carbon dioxide were conducted in semicrystalline BOPET at 35 °C. The solubility differs for all PET samples depending on the density, the microstructure and degree of crystallinity. It is generally assumed that semicrystalline polymers such as PET absorb low molar mass penetrants only in the amorphous phase, being their crystalline counterpart impermeable [52,65]. Solubility data are then normalized over the sample amorphous content. The concentration of carbon dioxide in BOPET measured in this work agrees with the results from Hirose et al. and is in line with the data from Keio Toi [56,62].

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The sorption isotherm of carbon dioxide in PPO was published in a previous work and is reported in Fig. 2 [66]. Stepwise sorption experiments of propane in amorphous PPO and semicrystalline BOPET were conducted. Solubility data are reported in Fig. 2B. Except for the system C3H8–PPO, the Langmuir contribution of the dual mode theory is sufficient to describe the sorption isotherms. The values of the model parameters are reported in Table 2. The solubility of carbon dioxide is two to three times greater in PPO than in BOPET while the concentration of propane in PPO is two to three orders of magnitude greater than in BOPET. The solubility of propane in PPO is in line with the results of Ilinich et al. measured at 20 °C [22,67]. The cavity diameters in the excess free volume of biaxially oriented PET and PPO have been previously estimated with Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopy and are equal to ~5 Å and ~6.7 Å respectively [51,68]. The kinetic diameters of carbon dioxide and propane are equal to 3.3 and 4.3 Å respectively. Therefore, both gases are expected to be hosted in the free volume voids within the two polymers.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
4.2. IR spectroscopy: sorption thermodynamics
The spectroscopic analysis starts from the sorption of propane in the two polymers. The polymer phase spectra of PPO before and after desorbing pure propane at 35 °C from ~1 bar are presented. The polymer absorbance in the interval [1100, 1150] cm-1 decreases when propane is absorbed. The contribution of propane to the IR spectrum is negligible in this frequency region and the absorbance variation is entirely attributed to the polymer [72–74]. The IR band is quite complex, but two peaks can be distinguished at 1115 and 1141 cm-1 separated by a broad IR band. The integrated absorbance is evaluated in the range [1100, 1150] cm-1 as a function of the penetrant concentration. At the minimum pressure investigated, the polymer spectrum is invariant. Propane is absorbed within the excess free volume cavities of the glassy polymer due to the predominance of the Langmuir sorption mode over Henry's. At greater penetrant concentrations, when the Henry sorption mode prevails, the integrated absorbance linearly decreases with the penetrant concentration.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: IR Spectroscopy in the transmission mode has proven capable of monitoring sorption induced swelling in rubbery crosslinked and glassy polymers [39,75]. In the system C3H8–PPO, only weak van der Waals interactions are present. Polymer dilation induced by propane, if any, is smaller than 2% at the maximum pressure investigated [75]. This result is further supported by the pure gas sorption isotherm: two stepwise sorption experiments conducted up to 0.537 and 0.930 bar after a first sorption run up to ~1 bar show invariant solubility. Therefore, in the analysis of diffusion kinetics, polymer swelling will be neglected. The spectra of propane and PPO are severely overlapped. To isolate the spectrum of propane in PPO, the polymer dry spectrum is subtracted from the polymer phase spectra collected at sorption equilibrium. Analytical IR signals for propane are identified and calibrated.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: When carbon dioxide is absorbed in the two polymers, two signals appear in the MIR region: the bending vibration in the range [600, 700] cm-1 and the antisymmetric stretching vibration at [2200, 2400] cm-1 [63,77–79]. Since carbon dioxide does not induce any structural modification of both polymers, the neat polymer spectrum is subtracted to isolate the signals of the penetrant in the polymer phase. As expected, the bending vibration splits into two separate signals centered at 654 and 659 cm-1 when CO2 is absorbed in BOPET due to weak specific interactions with the polymer carbonyl groups [78,79]. Carbon dioxide acts as an electron acceptor with respect to the oxygen of the carbonyl group forming Lewis acid-base complexes. Such a weak specific interaction is absent in PPO so that the bending vibration appears as a unique gaussian peak centered at 659 cm-1.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
4.3. Diffusion of pure gases
The diffusion of pure gases in PPO and BOPET is investigated with FTIR Spectroscopy in the transmission mode by monitoring the time evolution of the IR signal related to each penetrant in the polymer phase. Unary diffusion is theoretically described with the mass transport model and the M-S mass diffusivity are estimated from a non-linear least square fitting of the stepwise diffusion kinetics. To understand the effect of the thermodynamic factors on unary diffusion, they are evaluated as a function of the penetrant concentration. In the limit of infinite dilution, all systems are ideal and Γ equals unity. When the concentration increases, a typical bell-shaped curve describes the real polymer glassy phase. For carbon dioxide in BOPET or PPO, Γ does not reach a maximum because of the incomplete filling of the excess fractional free volume. The values of M-S mass diffusivity for unary gas diffusion in BOPET and PPO are reported. This parameter is related to the probability of a penetrant molecule to jump from one cavity to the other and is affected by the geometry and distribution of FFV cavities.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
4.4. Mixed gas sorption thermodynamics in PPO and BOPET
Mixed gas sorption experiments were conducted stepwise at 35 °C and sub-atmospheric pressure. The sorption isotherms are reported as a function of the species partial pressure in the gas phase. The concentration of carbon dioxide is depleted due to the simultaneous absorption of propane. Competitive sorption also occurs in BOPET although carbon dioxide is the most soluble penetrant. In the literature, for systems like CO2–CH4 in PPO and CO2–CH4 in PIM-1, the solubility of both penetrants decreases compared to pure gas sorption. Conversely, in PTMSP, only the solubility of methane drops significantly while that of carbon dioxide or butane is practically unaffected [28,83]. Experimental observations are discussed based on the dual mode theory. Mixed gas sorption is predicted with Eq. (6) based on the pure gas parameters. Because of the great affinity difference with PPO, the solubility of carbon dioxide reaches a plateau at ~0.1 bar while that of propane results unaffected.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The experimental data indicate that, when the partial pressure of propane is greater than ~0.1 bar, the solubility of carbon dioxide is reduced with respect to pure gas sorption but still increases linearly as a function of its partial pressure. The solubility of propane is also slightly promoted. This behavior is elucidated by considering that an increase of free volume is promoted when the Henry's sorption mode is activated by propane and carbon dioxide accompanied by conformational rearrangements within the polymer. The Henry's solubility coefficient kD,i is then a function of the polymer mixture composition. The values of the fitting parameter kD,i are reported in Table 2: for carbon dioxide, it is three times greater than the value found by Story and Koros for pure CO2 in PPO, confirming the positive effect of propane on the sorption of carbon dioxide [30]; that of propane also increases with respect to pure gas sorption by 30%.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
4.5. Multicomponent gas diffusion in PPO and BOPET
The co-diffusion of CO2–C3H8 in PPO and BOPET is analyzed. The diffusion kinetics measured during the integral sorption step up to 0.226 bar and at 35 °C and xCO2,∞=0.57 in PPO are reported. During the diffusion of carbon dioxide, the concentration overshoots the equilibrium loading at short times. Conversely, the kinetics of propane increases monotonically and is invariant with respect to pure gas diffusion. Therefore, the diffusion of propane is practically unaffected by the presence of carbon dioxide while the opposite holds true. The mass transport model is numerically solved to interpret the co-diffusion. The model requires the determination of the mobility and thermodynamic factors matrices. The former is simplified assuming a diluted system and small degree of correlation. The matrix of thermodynamic factors [Γ] is evaluated numerically. The off-diagonal elements of [Γ] are much different in magnitude from each other, and while Γii is always positive, Γij can be positive or negative depending on the concentration of the two components in the polymer.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The prediction of the co-diffusion of CO2–C3H8 in PPO is evaluated with static boundary conditions equal to the values measured at sorption equilibrium and with time-dependent boundary conditions measured spectroscopically. The model predicts the co-diffusion process excellently with static boundary conditions. The concentration overshoot in the diffusion kinetics of carbon dioxide is independent of polymer relaxation and results from mutual effects between the penetrants during co-diffusion, produced by the thermodynamic factors. The analysis of mass diffusion fluxes reveals that at the surface of the specimen, carbon dioxide is absorbed until a certain time, and then slowly desorbed. Peculiar phenomena named osmotic diffusion, reverse (uphill) diffusion, and barrier diffusion occur sequentially. This analysis outlines that by choosing the species chemical potential as the driving force for diffusion, mutual transport effects can be discriminated. The model overcomes the limits of the generalized Fick's law of diffusion.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: The time evolution of the real selectivity α of the membrane is outlined. At the beginning of the experiment, the fluxes of both components reach a maximum and the selectivity is the greatest. At short times this quantity plummets by two orders of magnitude. The selectivity becomes negligible approaching the time at which the concentration overshoot occurs. Clearly, the separation window is limited to very short times. Unsteady state separation operations can be attractive for niche applications as suggested in the literature [85]. Also, once the model has been validated, quantitative information on steady-state separation performances can be predicted as well. The diffusion kinetics of carbon dioxide during co-diffusion with propane in BOPET is also measured. The slope at short times slightly increases with respect to unary diffusion. The diffusion kinetics reaches a maximum at short times after which carbon dioxide is slowly desorbed. BOPET has a FFV much smaller than PPO, so further investigation is needed.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content:
5. Conclusions
The multicomponent diffusion of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous PPO and semicrystalline BOPET was investigated at 35 °C, sub-atmospheric pressure and mole fraction of carbon dioxide in the gas phase in the range 0.49 ÷ 0.57. The experiments were conducted in a closed volume coupling barometry and FTIR Spectroscopy in the transmission mode. The two polymer – penetrants systems are complementary. Carbon dioxide is the fastest diffusing species in both cases but its solubility compared with propane is greater in BOPET and smaller in PPO. The Dual Mode theory effectively described pure gas sorption thermodynamics and, when combined with the Maxwell – Stefan (M-S) transport theory, also unary gas diffusion. The analysis of the polymer phase infrared spectra confirmed that propane activates local conformational rearrangements of the polymer chains in PPO. Analytical infrared signals unaffected by the time evolution of the polymer spectrum were isolated and calibrated for each penetrant in PPO and BOPET.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate

content: Competitive sorption with propane depletes the concentration of carbon dioxide with respect to pure gas sorption at fixed partial pressure. In PPO, we modified the original Dual Mode model for binary gas mixtures to interpret the sorption thermodynamics. The Henry's sorption mode of CO2 is activated at very low pressures by propane due to the complete filling of the polymer FFV [83]. The analysis of multicomponent diffusion highlighted that only the diffusion of carbon dioxide is significantly affected by propane. Supra-equilibrium loading of carbon dioxide is measured at short times during multicomponent diffusion in both polymers, with greater magnitude in PPO. The M-S theory coupled with the Dual Mode sorption model predicts the concentration overshoot of carbon dioxide, interpreted as the sequential occurrence of osmotic – reverse – barrier diffusion. This phenomenon stems from the dependency of the species chemical potential on the concentration of the other diffusing penetrants. This work opens new opportunities for the exploitation of polymeric materials in unsteady separation operations.

citation.title: On the multicomponent diffusion of gases in polymer membranes: the case of CO2–C3H8 in amorphous polyphenylene oxide and semicrystalline polyethylene terephthalate
